revision.comment: Auto-generated page using script written by Immanuelle, either as a placeholder redirect or to make bots recognize the page as made by Immanuelle
revision.text: #redirect[[en:Matsuzawa_Yoshifusa]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

'''松沢 義章'''（まつざわ よしあきら、[[寛政]]3年（[[1791年]]） - [[万延]]2年（[[1861年]]）[[2月2日]]）は[[江戸時代]]後期の[[国学|国学者]]。通称は四郎左衛門。[[雅号|号]]は鵞湖山人、春秋逎舎など。
== 生涯 == [[信濃国]][[上諏訪宿]]で金銀、鼈甲を商う松沢四郎右衛門の子として生まれる。屋号は丸屋。先祖は[[飯田城 (信濃国)|飯田城下]]の商人で、江戸と諏訪、飯田の間を行商し、儒学を[[佐藤一斎]]に学ぶ。[[天保]]4年（1833年）43歳で[[平田篤胤]]に入門して国学を学ぶ。『[[春秋左氏伝]]』に造詣が深く、[[天保]]9年（1838年）「春秋大意」を著し、[[西晋]]の社預による「春秋経伝集解」に誤謬があることを指摘した。江戸では[[高野長英]]、[[最上徳内]]、[[平田銕胤]]、諏訪では[[飯田武郷]]らと親交を持った。また尊王を主唱し、諏訪から[[伊那谷]]にかけての[[天竜川]]筋の国学尊王論の祖と言われる。
多才で知られ、[[朝鮮語]]の研究も行い、[[ハングル|諺文]]で和歌を記したり、蒸気風呂釜を考案して[[江戸幕府|幕府]]に没収されるなどした。晩年は[[諏訪大社]]など地方史の研究も行い、「洲羽国考」、「洲羽古陵考」、「奉令集」、「顕幽本義」などの著書がある。万延2年（1861年）没。[[地蔵寺 (諏訪市)|地蔵寺]]に葬られた。
== 参考文献 == *[[市村咸人]] 『伊那尊王思想史』 1973年 *『日本人名大辞典』 講談社、2001年
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まつさわ よしあきら}} [[Category:国学者]] [[Category:信濃国の人物]] [[Category:1791年生]] [[Category:1861年没]]

{{Multiple issues |source=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |citation=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |sort_key=Person Died in 1861 }} '''Matsuzawa Yoshiakira''' (松沢 義章, 1791 - {{ill|March 12, 1861|ja|1861年}}) was a late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} {{ill|kokugaku|ja|国学}} scholar. His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in {{ill|Shimosuwa-shuku|ja|信濃国}}, a post station in the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)}} area, engaging in trade between {{ill|Edo|ja|江戸}}, Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒学}} under {{ill|Sato Issai|ja|佐藤一斎}}.
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4年}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}}, delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the {{ill|Spring and Autumn Annals|ja|春秋左氏伝}}, authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9年}} (1838), where he critiqued errors in {{ill|Du Yu|ja|杜預}}'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as {{ill|Takano Chōei|ja|高野長英}}, {{ill|Mogami Tokunai|ja|最上徳内}}, and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied {{ill|Korean language|ja|朝鮮語}}, composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the {{ill|Suwa Grand Shrine|ja|諏訪大社}} and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2年}} (1861) and was buried at {{ill|Jizō-ji|ja|地蔵寺}}.
== Notes == {{Citation help}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{ill|Ichimura Kansato|ja|市村咸人}}, *Ina Imperial Loyalist Thought History*, 1973. * *Japanese Biographical Dictionary*, Kodansha, 2001.
{{Normdaten}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Matsuzawa Yoshiakira}} [[Category:19th-century kokugaku scholars]] [[Category:People of Shinano Province]] [[Category:1791 births]] [[Category:1861 deaths]]
==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}[[Category:dd]][[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Suwa faith]] [[ja:松沢義章]][[Category:dd]][[Category:Suwa Faith topics in the template that need translation to English]] [[en:Matsuzawa Yoshifusa]][[Category:dd]][[Category:original pages]]

{{Multiple issues |source=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |citation=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |sort_key=Person Died in 1861 }} '''Matsuzawa Yoshiakira''' (松沢 義章, 1791 - {{ill|March 12, 1861|ja|1861年}}) was a late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} {{ill|kokugaku|ja|国学}} scholar. His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in {{ill|Shimosuwa-shuku|ja|信濃国}}, a post station in the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)}} area, engaging in trade between {{ill|Edo|ja|江戸}}, Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒学}} under {{ill|Sato Issai|ja|佐藤一斎}}.
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4年}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}}, delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the {{ill|Spring and Autumn Annals|ja|春秋左氏伝}}, authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9年}} (1838), where he critiqued errors in {{ill|Du Yu|ja|杜預}}'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as {{ill|Takano Chōei|ja|高野長英}}, {{ill|Mogami Tokunai|ja|最上徳内}}, and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied {{ill|Korean language|ja|朝鮮語}}, composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the {{ill|Suwa Grand Shrine|ja|諏訪大社}} and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2年}} (1861) and was buried at {{ill|Jizō-ji|ja|地蔵寺}}.
== Notes == {{Citation help}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{ill|Ichimura Kansato|ja|市村咸人}}, *Ina Imperial Loyalist Thought History*, 1973. * *Japanese Biographical Dictionary*, Kodansha, 2001.
{{Normdaten}} {{Japan-hist-stub}}

His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in {{ill|Shimosuwa-shuku|ja|信濃国}}, a post station in the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)}} area, engaging in trade between {{ill|Edo|ja|江戸}}, Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒学}} under {{ill|Sato Issai|ja|佐藤一斎}}.
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4年}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}}, delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the {{ill|Spring and Autumn Annals|ja|春秋左氏伝}}, authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9年}} (1838), where he critiqued errors in {{ill|Du Yu|ja|杜預}}'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as {{ill|Takano Chōei|ja|高野長英}}, {{ill|Mogami Tokunai|ja|最上徳内}}, and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied {{ill|Korean language|ja|朝鮮語}}, composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the {{ill|Suwa Grand Shrine|ja|諏訪大社}} and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2年}} (1861) and was buried at {{ill|Jizō-ji|ja|地蔵寺}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * {{ill|Ichimura Kansato|ja|市村咸人}}, *Ina Imperial Loyalist Thought History*, 1973. * *Japanese Biographical Dictionary*, Kodansha, 2001.
{{Normdaten}} {{Japan-hist-stub}}

{{Multiple issues |source=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |citation=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |sort_key=Person Died in 1861 }} '''Matsuzawa Yoshiakira''' (松沢 義章, 1791 - {{ill|March 12, 1861|ja|1861年}}) was a late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} {{ill|kokugaku|ja|国学}} scholar. His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in {{ill|Shimosuwa-shuku|ja|信濃国}}, a post station in the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)}} area, engaging in trade between {{ill|Edo|ja|江戸}}, Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒学}} under {{ill|Sato Issai|ja|佐藤一斎}}.
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4年}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}}, delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the {{ill|Spring and Autumn Annals|ja|春秋左氏伝}}, authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9年}} (1838), where he critiqued errors in {{ill|Du Yu|ja|杜預}}'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as {{ill|Takano Chōei|ja|高野長英}}, {{ill|Mogami Tokunai|ja|最上徳内}}, and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied {{ill|Korean language|ja|朝鮮語}}, composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the {{ill|Suwa Grand Shrine|ja|諏訪大社}} and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2年}} (1861) and was buried at {{ill|Jizō-ji|ja|地蔵寺}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * {{ill|Ichimura Kansato|ja|市村咸人}}, *Ina Imperial Loyalist Thought History*, 1973. * *Japanese Biographical Dictionary*, Kodansha, 2001.
{{Normdaten}} {{Japan-hist-stub}}

{{Multiple issues |source=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |citation=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |sort_key=Person Died in 1861 }} '''Matsuzawa Yoshiakira''' (松沢 義章, 1791 - {{ill|March 12, 1861|ja|1861年}}) was a late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} {{ill|kokugaku|ja|国学}} scholar. His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in {{ill|Shimosuwa-shuku|ja|信濃国}}, a post station in the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)}} area, engaging in trade between {{ill|Edo|ja|江戸}}, Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒学}} under {{ill|Sato Issai|ja|佐藤一斎}}.
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4年}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}}, delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the {{ill|Spring and Autumn Annals|ja|春秋左氏伝}}, authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9年}} (1838), where he critiqued errors in {{ill|Du Yu|ja|杜預}}'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as {{ill|Takano Chōei|ja|高野長英}}, {{ill|Mogami Tokunai|ja|最上徳内}}, and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied {{ill|Korean language|ja|朝鮮語}}, composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the {{ill|Suwa Grand Shrine|ja|諏訪大社}} and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2年}} (1861) and was buried at {{ill|Jizō-ji|ja|地蔵寺}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * {{ill|Ichimura Kansato|ja|市村咸人}}, *Ina Imperial Loyalist Thought History*, 1973. * *Japanese Biographical Dictionary*, Kodansha, 2001.

revision.text:
{{Multiple issues |source=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |citation=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |sort_key=Person Died in 1861 }} '''Matsuzawa Yoshiakira''' (松沢 義章, 1791 - {{ill|March 12, 1861|ja|1861年}}) was a late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} {{ill|kokugaku|ja|国学}} scholar. His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in {{ill|Shimosuwa-shuku|ja|信濃国}}, a post station in the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)}} area, engaging in trade between {{ill|Edo|ja|江戸}}, Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒学}} under {{ill|Sato Issai|ja|佐藤一斎}}.
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4年}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}}, delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the {{ill|Spring and Autumn Annals|ja|春秋左氏伝}}, authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9年}} (1838), where he critiqued errors in {{ill|Du Yu|ja|杜預}}'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as {{ill|Takano Chōei|ja|高野長英}}, {{ill|Mogami Tokunai|ja|最上徳内}}, and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied {{ill|Korean language|ja|朝鮮語}}, composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the {{ill|Suwa Grand Shrine|ja|諏訪大社}} and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2年}} (1861) and was buried at {{ill|Jizō-ji|ja|地蔵寺}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * {{ill|Ichimura Kansato|ja|市村咸人}}, *Ina Imperial Loyalist Thought History*, 1973. * *Japanese Biographical Dictionary*, Kodansha, 2001.
{{Normdaten}} {{Japan-hist-stub}}

{{Multiple issues |source=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |citation=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |sort_key=Person Died in 1861 }} '''Matsuzawa Yoshiakira''' (松沢 義章, 1791 - {{ill|March 12, 1861|ja|1861年}}) was a late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} {{ill|kokugaku|ja|国学}} scholar. His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in {{ill|Shimosuwa-shuku|ja|信濃国}}, a post station in the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)}} area, engaging in trade between {{ill|Edo|ja|江戸}}, Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒学}} under {{ill|Sato Issai|ja|佐藤一斎}}.
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4年}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}}, delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the {{ill|Spring and Autumn Annals|ja|春秋左氏伝}}, authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9年}} (1838), where he critiqued errors in {{ill|Du Yu|ja|杜預}}'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as {{ill|Takano Chōei|ja|高野長英}}, {{ill|Mogami Tokunai|ja|最上徳内}}, and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied {{ill|Korean language|ja|朝鮮語}}, composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the {{ill|Suwa Grand Shrine|ja|諏訪大社}} and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2年}} (1861) and was buried at {{ill|Jizō-ji|ja|地蔵寺}}.

{{Multiple issues |source=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |citation=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |sort_key=Person Died in 1861 }} '''Matsuzawa Yoshiakira''' (松沢 義章, 1791 - {{ill|March 12, 1861|ja|1861年}}) was a late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} {{ill|kokugaku|ja|国学}} scholar. His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in {{ill|Shimosuwa-shuku|ja|信濃国}}, a post station in the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)}} area, engaging in trade between {{ill|Edo|ja|江戸}}, Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒学}} under {{ill|Sato Issai|ja|佐藤一斎}}.
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4年}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}}, delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the {{ill|Spring and Autumn Annals|ja|春秋左氏伝}}, authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9年}} (1838), where he critiqued errors in {{ill|Du Yu|ja|杜預}}'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as {{ill|Takano Chōei|ja|高野長英}}, {{ill|Mogami Tokunai|ja|最上徳内}}, and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied {{ill|Korean language|ja|朝鮮語}}, composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the {{ill|Suwa Grand Shrine|ja|諏訪大社}} and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2年}} (1861) and was buried at {{ill|Jizō-ji|ja|地蔵寺}}.

{{Multiple issues |source=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |citation=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |sort_key=Person Died in 1861 }} '''Matsuzawa Yoshiakira''' (松沢 義章, 1791 - {{ill|March 12, 1861|ja|1861年}}) was a late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} {{ill|kokugaku|ja|国学}} scholar. His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in {{ill|Shimosuwa-shuku|ja|信濃国}}, a post station in the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)}} area, engaging in trade between {{ill|Edo|ja|江戸}}, Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒学}} under {{ill|Sato Issai|ja|佐藤一斎}}.
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4年}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}}, delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the {{ill|Spring and Autumn Annals|ja|春秋左氏伝}}, authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9年}} (1838), where he critiqued errors in {{ill|Du Yu|ja|杜預}}'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as {{ill|Takano Chōei|ja|高野長英}}, {{ill|Mogami Tokunai|ja|最上徳内}}, and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied {{ill|Korean language|ja|朝鮮語}}, composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the {{ill|Suwa Grand Shrine|ja|諏訪大社}} and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2年}} (1861) and was buried at {{ill|Jizō-ji|ja|地蔵寺}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * {{ill|Ichimura Kansato|ja|市村咸人}}, *Ina Imperial Loyalist Thought History*, 1973. * *Japanese Biographical Dictionary*, Kodansha, 2001.
{{Normdaten}} {{Japan-hist-stub}}

{{Multiple issues |source=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |citation=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |sort_key=Person Died in 1861 }} '''Matsuzawa Yoshiakira''' (松沢 義章, 1791 - {{ill|March 12, 1861|ja|1861年}}) was a late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} {{ill|kokugaku|ja|国学}} scholar. His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in {{ill|Shimosuwa-shuku|ja|信濃国}}, a post station in the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)}} area, engaging in trade between {{ill|Edo|ja|江戸}}, Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒学}} under {{ill|Sato Issai|ja|佐藤一斎}}.
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4年}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}}, delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the {{ill|Spring and Autumn Annals|ja|春秋左氏伝}}, authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9年}} (1838), where he critiqued errors in {{ill|Du Yu|ja|杜預}}'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as {{ill|Takano Chōei|ja|高野長英}}, {{ill|Mogami Tokunai|ja|最上徳内}}, and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied {{ill|Korean language|ja|朝鮮語}}, composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the {{ill|Suwa Grand Shrine|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2年}} (1861) and was buried at {{ill|Jizō-ji|ja|地蔵寺}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * {{ill|Ichimura Kansato|ja|市村咸人}}, *Ina Imperial Loyalist Thought History*, 1973. * *Japanese Biographical Dictionary*, Kodansha, 2001.
{{Normdaten}} {{Japan-hist-stub}}

{{Multiple issues |source=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |citation=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |sort_key=Person Died in 1861 }} '''Matsuzawa Yoshiakira''' (松沢 義章, 1791 - {{ill|March 12, 1861|ja|1861年}}) was a late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} {{ill|kokugaku|ja|国学}} scholar. His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in {{ill|Shimosuwa-shuku|ja|信濃国}}, a post station in the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)}} area, engaging in trade between {{ill|Edo|ja|江戸}}, Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒学}} under {{ill|Sato Issai|ja|佐藤一斎}}.
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4年}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}}, delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the {{ill|Spring and Autumn Annals|ja|春秋左氏伝}}, authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9年}} (1838), where he critiqued errors in {{ill|Du Yu|ja|杜預}}'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as {{ill|Takano Chōei|ja|高野長英}}, {{ill|Mogami Tokunai|ja|最上徳内}}, and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied {{ill|Korean language|ja|朝鮮語}}, composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the {{ill|Suwa Grand Shrine|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2年}} (1861) and was buried at {{ill|Jizō-ji|ja|地蔵寺}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * {{ill|Ichimura Kansato|ja|市村咸人}}, *Ina Imperial Loyalist Thought History*, 1973. * *Japanese Biographical Dictionary*, Kodansha, 2001.

{{Multiple issues |source=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |citation=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |sort_key=Person Died in 1861 }} '''Matsuzawa Yoshiakira''' (松沢 義章, 1791 - {{ill|March 12, 1861|ja|1861年}}) was a late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} {{ill|kokugaku|ja|国学}} scholar. His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in {{ill|Shimosuwa-shuku|ja|信濃国}}, a post station in the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)}} area, engaging in trade between {{ill|Edo|ja|江戸}}, Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒学}} under {{ill|Sato Issai|ja|佐藤一斎}}.
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4年}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}}, delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the {{ill|Spring and Autumn Annals|ja|春秋左氏伝}}, authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9年}} (1838), where he critiqued errors in {{ill|Du Yu|ja|杜預}}'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as {{ill|Takano Chōei|ja|高野長英}}, {{ill|Mogami Tokunai|ja|最上徳内}}, and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied {{ill|Korean language|ja|朝鮮語}}, composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the {{ill|Suwa Grand Shrine|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2年}} (1861) and was buried at {{ill|Jizō-ji|ja|地蔵寺}}.

{{Multiple issues |source=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |citation=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |sort_key=Person Died in 1861 }} '''Matsuzawa Yoshiakira''' (松沢 義章, 1791 - {{ill|March 12, 1861|ja|1861st year }}) was a late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} {{ill|kokugaku|ja|国学}} scholar. His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in {{ill|Shimosuwa-shuku|ja|信濃国}}, a post station in the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)}} area, engaging in trade between {{ill|Edo|ja|江戸}}, Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒学}} under {{ill|Sato Issai|ja|佐藤一斎}}.
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4年}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}}, delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the {{ill|Spring and Autumn Annals|ja|春秋左氏伝}}, authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9年}} (1838), where he critiqued errors in {{ill|Du Yu|ja|杜預}}'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as {{ill|Takano Chōei|ja|高野長英}}, {{ill|Mogami Tokunai|ja|最上徳内}}, and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied {{ill|Korean language|ja|朝鮮語}}, composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the {{ill|Suwa Grand Shrine|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2nd year }} (1861) and was buried at {{ill|Jizō-ji|ja|地蔵寺}}.

{{Multiple issues |source=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |citation=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |sort_key=Person Died in 1861 }} '''Matsuzawa Yoshiakira''' (松沢 義章, 1791 - {{ill|March 12, 1861|ja|1861st year }}) was a late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} {{ill|kokugaku|ja|国学}} scholar. His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in {{ill|Shimosuwa-shuku|ja|信濃国}}, a post station in the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)}} area, engaging in trade between {{ill|Edo|ja|江戸}}, Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒学}} under {{ill|Sato Issai|ja|佐藤一斎}}.
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4th year }} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}}, delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the {{ill|Spring and Autumn Annals|ja|春秋左氏伝}}, authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9th year }} (1838), where he critiqued errors in {{ill|Du Yu|ja|杜預}}'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as {{ill|Takano Chōei|ja|高野長英}}, {{ill|Mogami Tokunai|ja|最上徳内}}, and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied {{ill|Korean language|ja|朝鮮語}}, composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the {{ill|Suwa Grand Shrine|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2nd year }} (1861) and was buried at {{ill|Jizō-ji|ja|地蔵寺}}.

{{Multiple issues |source=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |citation=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |sort_key=Person Died in 1861 }} '''Matsuzawa Yoshiakira''' (松沢 義章, 1791 - {{ill|March 12, 1861|ja|1861st year|12=draft}}) was a late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}} {{ill|kokugaku|ja|国学|12=draft}} scholar. His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in {{ill|Shimosuwa-shuku|ja|信濃国|12=draft}}, a post station in the {{ill|Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方|12=draft}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)|12=draft}} area, engaging in trade between {{ill|Edo|ja|江戸|12=draft}}, Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒学|12=draft}} under {{ill|Sato Issai|ja|佐藤一斎|12=draft}}.
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4th year|12=draft}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤|12=draft}}, delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the {{ill|Spring and Autumn Annals|ja|春秋左氏伝|12=draft}}, authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9th year|12=draft}} (1838), where he critiqued errors in {{ill|Du Yu|ja|杜預|12=draft}}'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as {{ill|Takano Chōei|ja|高野長英|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mogami Tokunai|ja|最上徳内|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤|12=draft}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷|12=draft}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川|12=draft}} region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷|12=draft}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied {{ill|Korean language|ja|朝鮮語|12=draft}}, composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文|12=draft}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|12=draft}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the {{ill|Suwa Grand Shrine|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|12=draft}} and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2nd year|12=draft}} (1861) and was buried at {{ill|Jizō-ji|ja|地蔵寺|12=draft}}.
== Notes ==

Matsuzawa studied [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]] under [[:en:Satō Issai|Sato Issai]].
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō 4|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of [[:en:Hirata Atsutane|Hirata Atsutane]], delving into kokugaku. In Edo, he associated with figures such as [[:en:Takano Chōei|Takano Chōei]], [[:en:Mogami Tokunai|Mogami Tokunai]], and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirata Kanetane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平田鐵胤|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平田鐵胤|1=Hirata Kanetane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537446}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Takekuni|qq=|1=Iida Takesato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535088}}. Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied [[:en:Korean language|Korean language]], composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hangul|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハングル|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハングル|1=Hangul|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8222}}. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:松沢義章]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1791|death_date=1861|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|WD=Q205662}}|occupation={{ill|Kokugaku scholar|WD=Q96926046}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q105711491]] Wikidata name: Matsuzawa Yoshiakira [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:05, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Multiple issues |source=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |citation=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |sort_key=Person Died in 1861 }} '''Matsuzawa Yoshiakira''' (松沢 義章, 1791 - [[:en:1861|March 12, 1861]]) was a late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Kokugaku|kokugaku]] scholar. His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in [[:en:Shinano Province|Shimosuwa-shuku]], a post station in the {{ill|Suwa Region|lt=Suwa Region|lt=Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa region|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪地域|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪地域}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida-jō|qq=|1=Iida Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666503}} area, engaging in trade between [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]] under [[:en:Satō Issai|Sato Issai]].
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō 4|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of [[:en:Hirata Atsutane|Hirata Atsutane]], delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the [[:en:Zuo Zhuan|Spring and Autumn Annals]], authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō 9|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1838), where he critiqued errors in [[:en:Du Yu|Du Yu]]'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as [[:en:Takano Chōei|Takano Chōei]], [[:en:Mogami Tokunai|Mogami Tokunai]], and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirata Kanetane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平田鐵胤|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平田鐵胤|1=Hirata Kanetane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537446}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Takekuni|qq=|1=Iida Takesato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535088}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the [[:en:Tenryū River|Tenryū River]] region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina Valley|qq=}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied [[:en:Korean language|Korean language]], composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hangul|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハングル|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハングル|1=Hangul|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8222}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Man'en 2|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1861年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1861年|1=1861|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7693}} (1861) and was buried at [[:en:Dizang Temple|Jizō-ji]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:松沢義章]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1791|death_date=1861|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|WD=Q205662}}|occupation={{ill|Kokugaku scholar|WD=Q96926046}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q105711491]] Wikidata name: Matsuzawa Yoshiakira [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:05, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Multiple issues |source=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |citation=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |sort_key=Person Died in 1861 }} '''Matsuzawa Yoshiakira''' (松沢 義章, 1791 - [[:en:1861|March 12, 1861]]) was a late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Kokugaku|kokugaku]] scholar. His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in [[:en:Shinano Province|Shimosuwa-shuku]], a post station in the {{ill|Suwa Region|lt=Suwa Region|lt=Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪地域|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪地域}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida-jō|1=Iida Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666503}} area, engaging in trade between [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]] under [[:en:Satō Issai|Sato Issai]].
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of [[:en:Hirata Atsutane|Hirata Atsutane]], delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the [[:en:Zuo Zhuan|Spring and Autumn Annals]], authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō 9|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1838), where he critiqued errors in [[:en:Du Yu|Du Yu]]'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as [[:en:Takano Chōei|Takano Chōei]], [[:en:Mogami Tokunai|Mogami Tokunai]], and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirata Kanetane|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平田鐵胤|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平田鐵胤|1=Hirata Kanetane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537446}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Takekuni|1=Iida Takesato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535088}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the [[:en:Tenryū River|Tenryū River]] region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina Valley|qq=}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied [[:en:Korean language|Korean language]], composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hangul|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハングル|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハングル|1=Hangul|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8222}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Man'en 2|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1861年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1861年|1=1861|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7693}} (1861) and was buried at [[:en:Dizang Temple|Jizō-ji]].
== Notes ==

{{Japan-hist-stub}}

His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in [[:en:Shinano Province|Shimosuwa-shuku]], a post station in the {{ill|Suwa Region|lt=Suwa Region|lt=Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪地域|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪地域}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida-jō|1=Iida Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666503}} area, engaging in trade between [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]] under [[:en:Satō Issai|Sato Issai]].
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of [[:en:Hirata Atsutane|Hirata Atsutane]], delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the [[:en:Zuo Zhuan|Spring and Autumn Annals]], authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō 9|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1838), where he critiqued errors in [[:en:Du Yu|Du Yu]]'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as [[:en:Takano Chōei|Takano Chōei]], [[:en:Mogami Tokunai|Mogami Tokunai]], and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirata Kanetane|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平田鐵胤|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平田鐵胤|1=Hirata Kanetane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537446}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Takekuni|1=Iida Takesato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535088}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the [[:en:Tenryū River|Tenryū River]] region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina Valley|qq=}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied [[:en:Korean language|Korean language]], composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hangul|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハングル|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハングル|1=Hangul|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8222}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Man'en 2|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1861年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1861年|1=1861|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7693}} (1861) and was buried at [[:en:Dizang Temple|Jizō-ji]].

His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in [[:en:Shinano Province|Shimosuwa-shuku]], a post station in the {{ill|Suwa Region|lt=Suwa Region|lt=Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪地域|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪地域}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida-jō|1=Iida Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666503}} area, engaging in trade between [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]] under [[:en:Satō Issai|Sato Issai]].
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of [[:en:Hirata Atsutane|Hirata Atsutane]], delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the [[:en:Zuo Zhuan|Spring and Autumn Annals]], authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō 9|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1838), where he critiqued errors in [[:en:Du Yu|Du Yu]]'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as [[:en:Takano Chōei|Takano Chōei]], [[:en:Mogami Tokunai|Mogami Tokunai]], and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirata Kanetane|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平田鐵胤|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平田鐵胤|1=Hirata Kanetane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537446}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Takekuni|1=Iida Takesato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535088}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the [[:en:Tenryū River|Tenryū River]] region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina Valley|qq=}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied [[:en:Korean language|Korean language]], composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hangul|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハングル|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハングル|1=Hangul|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8222}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Man'en 2|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1861年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1861年|1=1861|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7693}} (1861) and was buried at [[:en:Dizang Temple|Jizō-ji]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:松沢義章]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:松沢義章]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:松沢義章]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1791|death_date=1861|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|WD=Q205662}}|occupation={{ill|Kokugaku scholar|WD=Q96926046}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q105711491]] Wikidata name: Matsuzawa Yoshiakira [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:05, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Multiple issues |source=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |citation=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |sort_key=Person Died in 1861 }} '''Matsuzawa Yoshiakira''' (松沢 義章, 1791 - [[:en:1861|March 12, 1861]]) was a late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Kokugaku|kokugaku]] scholar. His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in [[:en:Shinano Province|Shimosuwa-shuku]], a post station in the {{ill|Suwa Region|lt=Suwa Region|lt=Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪地域|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪地域}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida-jō|1=Iida Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666503}} area, engaging in trade between [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]] under [[:en:Satō Issai|Sato Issai]].
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of [[:en:Hirata Atsutane|Hirata Atsutane]], delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the [[:en:Zuo Zhuan|Spring and Autumn Annals]], authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō 9|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1838), where he critiqued errors in [[:en:Du Yu|Du Yu]]'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as [[:en:Takano Chōei|Takano Chōei]], [[:en:Mogami Tokunai|Mogami Tokunai]], and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirata Kanetane|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平田鐵胤|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平田鐵胤|1=Hirata Kanetane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537446}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Takekuni|1=Iida Takesato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535088}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the [[:en:Tenryū River|Tenryū River]] region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina Valley|qq=}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied [[:en:Korean language|Korean language]], composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hangul|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハングル|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハングル|1=Hangul|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8222}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Man'en 2|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1861年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1861年|1=1861|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7693}} (1861) and was buried at [[:en:Dizang Temple|Jizō-ji]].

{{Japan-hist-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:松沢義章]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:松沢義章]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:松沢義章]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1791|death_date=1861|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|WD=Q205662}}|occupation={{ill|Kokugaku scholar|WD=Q96926046}}}} {{Multiple issues |source=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |citation=January 30, 2021 (Saturday) 20:17 (UTC) |sort_key=Person Died in 1861 }} '''Matsuzawa Yoshiakira''' (松沢 義章, 1791 - {{ill|1861|en|1861|WD=Q7693|lt=March 12, 1861}}) was a late {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=kokugaku}} scholar. His common name was Shirōzaemon, and his pen names included Gako Sanjin and Shunju no Sha.
== Life == Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shimosuwa-shuku}}, a post station in the {{ill|Suwa Region|lt=Suwa Region|lt=Suwa region|ja|諏訪地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪地域|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪地域}}, Matsuzawa was the son of Shirōzaemon, a merchant dealing in gold, silver, and tortoiseshell. The family shop, known as Maruya, had ancestral roots in the {{ill|Iida-jō|ja|飯田城 (信濃国)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida-jō|1=Iida Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666503}} area, engaging in trade between {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}}, Suwa, and Iida. Matsuzawa studied {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|lt=Confucianism}} under {{ill|Satō Issai|en|Satō Issai|WD=Q6463901|lt=Sato Issai}}.
At the age of 43 in {{ill|Tenpō 4|ja|天保4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1833), Matsuzawa became a disciple of {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|en|Hirata Atsutane|WD=Q2572749|lt=Hirata Atsutane}}, delving into kokugaku. He demonstrated deep knowledge of the {{ill|Zuo Zhuan|en|Zuo Zhuan|WD=Q230230|lt=Spring and Autumn Annals}}, authoring *Shunju Taii* in {{ill|Tenpō 9|ja|天保9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō 9|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1838), where he critiqued errors in {{ill|Du Yu|en|Du Yu|WD=Q702166|lt=Du Yu}}'s *Chunqiu Jingzhuan Jiji*. In Edo, he associated with figures such as {{ill|Takano Chōei|en|Takano Chōei|WD=Q944693|lt=Takano Chōei}}, {{ill|Mogami Tokunai|en|Mogami Tokunai|WD=Q4141812|lt=Mogami Tokunai}}, and {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirata Kanetane|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平田鐵胤|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平田鐵胤|1=Hirata Kanetane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537446}}, while in Suwa, he maintained ties with {{ill|Iida Takekuni|ja|飯田武郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Takekuni|1=Iida Takesato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6535088}}. Matsuzawa was a proponent of imperial loyalty, becoming a foundational figure for kokugaku and imperial loyalist thought in the {{ill|Tenryū River|en|Tenryū River|WD=Q13667|lt=Tenryū River}} region between Suwa and {{ill|Ina Valley|ja|伊那谷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina Valley|qq=}}.
Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied {{ill|Korean language|en|Korean language|WD=Q9176|lt=Korean language}}, composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hangul|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハングル|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハングル|1=Hangul|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8222}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha}} and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Man'en 2|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1861年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1861年|1=1861|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7693}} (1861) and was buried at {{ill|Dizang Temple|en|Dizang Temple|WD=Q1231809|lt=Jizō-ji}}.

Known for his versatility, Matsuzawa also studied {{ill|Korean language|en|Korean language|WD=Q9176|lt=Korean language|1=Korean language}}, composing Japanese waka in {{ill|Hangul|ja|諺文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hangul|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハングル|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハングル|1=Hangul|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8222}}. He designed a steam bath kettle that was confiscated by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In his later years, Matsuzawa turned his attention to local history, researching topics related to the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}} and writing works such as *Suwa Kunikō*, *Suwa Koryōkō*, *Hōreishū*, and *Ken'yū Hongi*. He passed away in {{ill|Man'en 2|ja|万延2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Man'en 2|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1861年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1861年|1=1861|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7693}} (1861) and was buried at {{ill|Dizang Temple|en|Dizang Temple|WD=Q1231809|lt=Jizō-ji|1=Dizang Temple}}.
